comment.body:
and so the strategy here besides thinks like intraday vol selling, or going longer vol at certain strikes, etc... is that the call side is underpriced while the put side is crowded.
they are constantly squeezed out while the market goes down. this is why the IC whale tries to capitalize on this, but the fluctuation is just big. it is a trending rangebound basically

the key is: always have a concept, always have a plan. be stick to it, be open-minded,

write it down on a paper, and have a plan

user.bio: Just trying to figure out the puzzle of financial markets

comment.body:
During the morning obviously you’ll have the most premium to collect but the most risk, I try to wait for the 1st hour to come and go but recently they’ve been so juiced I’m able to place trades in the morning far enough away. 

I usually try to pick a credit spread 2%+ away from currently price, especially if we open up or down .5% ish. I have some statistics on 30 years of NDX data and only about 5% of days close down -2.7% or more. I’ve built a VaR model and GJR-GARCH model as well and am working on my own intraday volatility model based on Alma’s concepts. Obviously I analyze Alma’s posts in the morning and then start looking for strikes well beyond where we’re likely to go. I also look at the implied move for the day. Tomorrow’s is supposed to be 90 points on SPX (1.6%). 

I cut it extremely close today, but that’s essentially what I’ve been doing. Do what you will with it. I may not even play anything tomorrow. I’ve always had an affinity with the Nasdaq more than the S&P, you can do either or. My $0.02
